---
title: "AIRR Community Meeting Tutorial: Joint Analysis of AIRR and scRNA-seq data in R"
author: "Burhan Sabuwala, Jian Xing"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: 
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 1
    toc_float: true
    number_sections: true
    theme: cosmo
---



```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE)
```

# Overview

This tutorial walks through integrating single-cell RNA-seq (scRNA-seq) gene expression data with B cell receptor (BCR) V(D)J repertoire data stored in [AIRR format](https://docs.airr-community.org/en/stable/). By the end of this tutorial, you will be able to:

- Load and align AIRR-format BCR repertoire data with a Seurat single-cell object
- Calculate somatic hypermutation (SHM) frequency and CDR3 properties for each B cell
- Visualize BCR features on Gene expression based UMAP embeddings
- Jointly analyze transcriptional and repertoire features to identify B cell subsets and their maturation states

**Key concepts:**

- **AIRR format**: A standardized TSV format for adaptive immune receptor rearrangement data. The `airr` R package reads these files directly.
- **SHM (Somatic Hypermutation)**: Mutations introduced into BCR variable regions during affinity maturation in germinal centers. Higher `mu_freq` = more mature/antigen-experienced B cell.
- **Isotype**: The constant region of the BCR heavy chain (IgM, IgD, IgG, IgA, IgE). Class-switching from IgM to IgG/IgA reflects B cell activation.
- **CDR3**: The complementarity-determining region 3 is the most variable part of the BCR and critical for antigen binding. CDR3 length and amino acid properties can indicate functional differences between B cell subsets.

## Prerequisites

The following R packages are required:

| Package | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `Seurat` | Single-cell RNA-seq analysis and object management |
| `zellkonverter` | Reading and converting `.h5ad` (AnnData) files into Seurat-compatible objects |
| `dplyr` | Data manipulation |
| `ggplot2` | Data visualization |
| `ggpubr` | Statistical annotations for ggplot2 figures | 
| `shazam` | Somatic hypermutation frequency analysis from the Immcantaion suite|
| `alakazam` | BCR lineage and repertoire analysis from the Immcantation suite|
| `airr` | Reading AIRR-format rearrangement files |



The subject for this tutorial is Y3, a young donor given seasonal influenza vaccination. Samples are collected at Day 0 (pre-vaccination) and Day 7 (post-vaccination). The Seurat object is loaded from an .h5ad file and contains UMAP embeddings, cluster assignments, and cell type annotations from CellTypist. The AIRR rearrangement file contains BCR sequences with V(D)J gene calls, isotype information, and mutation frequencies.



# Set up

## Load Libraries

```{r libraries}
library(Seurat)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(shazam)
library(zellkonverter)
library(alakazam)
```


## Load data and annotate samples

The single-cell object is read from an .h5ad file using zellkonverter and converted to a Seurat object. Subject and sample name metadata are then added directly, with timepoint extracted from the sample name.

```{r}
sce <- readH5AD("concat.h5ad")
obj <- as.Seurat(sce, counts = "X", data = "X")
```


```{r}
obj$subject <- "Y3"
obj$sample_name <- NA
obj$sample_name[obj$sample == "F15_0_filtered_feature_bc_matrix"] <- "Y3_0"
obj$sample_name[obj$sample == "F15_7_filtered_feature_bc_matrix"] <- "Y3_7"
obj$timepoint <- sub("Y3_", "", obj$sample_name)
```


## Load AIRR Data

The AIRR rearrangement file is loaded using `airr::read_rearrangement()`. This file contains BCR V(D)J sequences for all cells in the dataset.


```{r load single cell object}
airr_data <- airr::read_rearrangement("airrc_shaw_singleCell.tsv")
```


### Calculate Somatic Hypermutation (SHM) Frequency

`observedMutations()` from the **shazam** package computes per-sequence mutation frequency (`mu_freq`) by comparing each BCR sequence to its inferred germline. This is a core metric for B cell maturation state — naïve B cells have low SHM while germinal center and memory B cells accumulate more mutations.

```{r mutation freq}
# Shazam function
airr_data <- observedMutations(airr_data,
                                sequenceColumn = "sequence_alignment", 
                                germlineColumn = "germline_alignment_d_mask",
                                regionDefinition = NULL, frequency = TRUE,
                                combine = TRUE, nproc = 4)
```

### Calculate CDR3 Amino Acid Properties

```{r amino acid properties}
# alakazam function
airr_data <- aminoAcidProperties(airr_data, seq = "junction", trim = TRUE, label = "cdr3")
```

### Merge AIRR Metadata into the Seurat Object

Only **heavy chain (IGH)** productive rearrangements are retained for merging, as these carry isotype and SHM information. Cell barcodes are reconstructed by concatenating `cell_id` with the sample name to match the gene expression object barcode format. The following AIRR fields are extracted per cell and added to the Seurat object's metadata via `AddMetaData()`:

| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| `v_call`, `d_call`, `j_call` | V, D, J gene assignments |
| `mu_freq` | Somatic hypermutation frequency |
| `c_call` | Isotype (e.g., IGHM, IGHA, IGHG) |
| `clone_id` | Clonal cluster assignment |
| `cdr3_aa_length` | CDR3 length |

A boolean column `has_airr` is added to flag cells that have a matched BCR sequence. Not all cells in a scRNA-seq experiment will have BCR data (due to dropout or low-quality reads), so always check coverage before interpreting repertoire results.

```{r combine airr data}
# For tutorial we are only consider the heavy chain and productive rearrangements for merging with the Seurat metadata.
airr_heavy <- airr_data %>%
  filter(productive == TRUE) %>%
  filter(locus == "IGH")

# Concatenating cell_id with sample_name to create a unique identifier for merging with Seurat metadata
airr_heavy <- airr_heavy %>%
  mutate(
    cell_barcode = paste0(cell_id, "-F15_", day, "_filtered_feature_bc_matrix")
  ) 

airr_meta <- airr_heavy %>%
  select(
    cell_id,
    cell_barcode,
    productive,
    v_call,
    d_call,
    j_call,
    c_call, # Isotype
    mu_freq,
    cdr3_aa_length,
    clone_id,
    indels,
    sequence_vdj,
    cdr1, 
    cdr2,
    consensus_count
         ) %>%
  distinct(cell_barcode, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
  tibble::column_to_rownames("cell_barcode")

obj <- AddMetaData(obj, metadata = airr_meta)

obj$has_airr <- rownames(obj@meta.data) %in% rownames(airr_meta)

table(obj$has_airr)
```

# Visualization

## Cell Type Annotations

The plot below shows the UMAP embedding colored by cell type annotations from CellTypist (`cell_type_majority_vote`). Four B cell populations are of primary interest: Naïve B cells, Memory B cells, Age-associated B cells, and Plasma cells.

```{r}
DimPlot(obj, group.by = "cell_type_majority_vote", label = TRUE) + NoLegend()
```

## BCR Coverage

This plot shows which cells on the UMAP have a matched BCR V(D)J sequence (`has_airr` = `TRUE`). Systematic gaps in coverage may indicate technical dropout or that certain cell types (e.g., plasma cells) produce fewer recoverable sequences.

```{r}
DimPlot(obj, group.by = "has_airr", pt.size = 0.3) + labs(title = "Has AIRR data?") + theme_classic()
```

## SHM Frequency on UMAP

Projecting mutation frequency onto the UMAP spatially relates mutational burden with transcriptional state. Clusters with high `mu_freq` correspond to more antigen-experienced B cells (e.g., memory B cells, plasmablasts).

```{r}
FeaturePlot(obj, features = "mu_freq", pt.size = 0.3, order = TRUE)
```


## Isotype Distribution on UMAP

Coloring the UMAP by isotype (`c_call`) reveals whether particular transcriptional clusters are dominated by specific isotypes, which can indicate functional B cell states (e.g., a plasma cell cluster enriched for IgA or IgG).

```{r}
DimPlot(obj, group.by = "c_call", pt.size = 0.3) + labs(title = "Isotype") + theme_classic() 
```

## SHM frequency and isotype distribution by cell type

Mean SHM frequency and isotype composition are compared across the four B cell populations. Expected biology: Naïve B cells should show low SHM and be predominantly IgM/IgD, while Memory B cells and Plasma cells should show higher SHM and enrichment for class-switched isotypes (IgG, IgA).

```{r}
# Define B cell types and isotypes of interest
b_cell_types <- c("Plasma cells", "Naive B cells", "Age-associated B cells", "Memory B cells")
isotypes_of_interest <- c("IGHM", "IGHD", "IGHA", "IGHG", "IGHE")

# Subset metadata to B cells with AIRR data
b_meta <- obj@meta.data %>%
  filter(cell_type_majority_vote %in% b_cell_types, has_airr == TRUE) %>%
  mutate(
    celltype = cell_type_majority_vote,
    isotype  = substr(c_call, 1, 4)
  )

# --- Plot 1: Mean SHM Frequency per Cell Type ---
b_meta %>%
  filter(!is.na(mu_freq)) %>%
  group_by(celltype) %>%
  summarise(mean_mu = mean(mu_freq), se_mu = sd(mu_freq) / sqrt(n())) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = celltype, y = mean_mu, fill = celltype)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mean_mu - se_mu, ymax = mean_mu + se_mu), width = 0.2) +
  labs(title = "Mean SHM Frequency by B Cell Type",
       x = NULL, y = "Mean Mutation Frequency") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))

# --- Plot 2: Isotype Distribution per Cell Type ---
b_meta %>%
  filter(isotype %in% isotypes_of_interest) %>%
  group_by(celltype, isotype) %>%
  summarise(n = n(), .groups = "drop") %>%
  group_by(celltype) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n) * 100) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = celltype, y = pct, fill = isotype)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6) +
  labs(title = "Isotype Distribution by B Cell Type",
       x = NULL, y = "Percentage of Cells (%)") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))
```


## Cell type composition, SHM frequency, and isotype distribution by timepoint

Here we compare transcriptional and repertoire features between Day 0 and Day 7 across the four B cell populations. Expected post-vaccination biology includes: an increase in Plasma cells at Day 7, higher SHM frequency in class-switched populations, and a shift from IgM-dominated to IgG/IgA-dominated isotype profiles in Memory B cells and Plasma cells.

```{r}

# --- Setup ---
cluster_meta <- obj@meta.data %>%
  filter(has_airr == TRUE) %>%
  mutate(
    timepoint_label = ifelse(timepoint == "0", "Day 0", "Day 7"),
    isotype = substr(c_call, 1, 4),
    cluster = cell_type_majority_vote
  ) %>%
  filter(cluster %in% b_cell_types)

# --- Plot 1: Mutation Frequency per cluster, split by timepoint ---
cluster_meta %>%
  filter(!is.na(mu_freq)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = timepoint_label, y = mu_freq, fill = timepoint_label)) +
  geom_violin(alpha = 0.7, trim = FALSE) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.12, outlier.shape = NA, fill = "white") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Day 0" = "steelblue", "Day 7" = "firebrick")) +
  facet_wrap(~cluster, nrow = 1) +
  ggpubr::stat_compare_means(method = "wilcox.test", label = "p.signif",
                     label.x = 1.5) +
  labs(title = "SHM Frequency by Cell Type: Day 0 vs Day 7",
       x = NULL, y = "Mutation Frequency") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        strip.text = element_text(size = 8))
```

Characterizing the shift in cell type composition between Day 0 and Day 7.
```{r}
# --- Plot 2: Cell type composition shift per timepoint ---
cluster_meta %>%
  count(timepoint_label, cluster) %>%
  group_by(timepoint_label) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n) * 100) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = timepoint_label, y = pct, fill = cluster)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.1f%%", pct)),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5), size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Cell Type Composition: Day 0 vs Day 7",
       x = NULL, y = "Percentage (%)", fill = "Cell Type") +
  theme_bw()
```



```{r}
# --- Plot 3: Cell type absolute counts per timepoint ---
cluster_meta %>%
  count(timepoint_label, cluster) %>%
  group_by(timepoint_label) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n) * 100) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = cluster, y = pct, fill = timepoint_label)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.1f%%", pct)),
            position = position_dodge(width = 0.6),
            vjust = -0.4, size = 3) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Day 0" = "steelblue", "Day 7" = "firebrick")) +
  labs(title = "Cell Type Composition: Day 0 vs Day 7",
       x = NULL, y = "% of Cells", fill = "Timepoint") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))
```

Isotype distribution shift between Day 0 and Day 7 across the four B cell populations.
```{r}
cluster_meta %>%
  filter(isotype %in% c("IGHM", "IGHD", "IGHA", "IGHG", "IGHE"),
         !is.na(isotype)) %>%
  count(timepoint_label, cluster, isotype) %>%
  group_by(timepoint_label, cluster) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n) * 100) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = timepoint_label, y = pct, fill = isotype)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6) +
  facet_wrap(~cluster, nrow = 1) +
  labs(title = "Isotype Distribution per Cell Type: Day 0 vs Day 7",
       x = NULL, y = "Percentage (%)", fill = "Isotype") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1),
        strip.text = element_text(size = 8))
```
